## Real Material - Architect Learning Build (Storage: S3 Foundations)

This section is the study-first architecture guide for s3 foundations, buckets, objects, policies, hosting, and class selection.

### Phase 1 - Foundations

#### Module: S3 Foundations Mental Model
- Use s3 foundations decisions to shape blast radius, access paths, and operational ownership.
- Study service behavior first, then map it into production architecture and exam-ready design language.

- So, for example, Cloudtrail logs would indicate somebody making a change to a bucket like configuring encryption or changing the storage type, but it wouldn't log an upload made by a user outside of your account or someone on the internet accessing an object from like a gaming application that wouldn't be logged by Cloudtrail event logging, but it would be logged by server access logs.
- So if we had all of our images inside a prefix called images inside of our bucket and we didn't want to give access to anything else in our bucket except for our images, well, we could change the resource to simply include everything under the prefix of images, and folks on the internet would only be able to retrieve files and objects that lived inside of the images prefix there.
- So even if we were trying to modify individual buckets and make individual objects or the bucket itself public, even if we changed or turned off block public access per bucket, well, Amazon is still going to block us from making any changes to our policy that permits public access because the account level settings are going to proceed over top of our individual bucket settings.
- So this may be handy if you're using applications and you have configured some apps to point to a specific bucket or some CI CD pipelines or something like that, you can simply keep the name and then when you drop objects inside of that bucket, then you can obviously have your application or your pipeline continue to use that same bucket.
- Well, we can retrieve a specific version of an object as needed from an S3 bucket that has versioning enabled a regular gets just a traditional get against that object is going to retrieve the latest version if that get also specifies the version of that object, we're going to get that specific version of the object.
- So again, a little bit more complex, not too crazy, but again, I want to showcase how we continue adding more and more to our policy to get more fine grained controls specifically for our bucket and maybe objects in our bucket and of course limit the actions that a specific user can perform on those resources.
- So if you have a lot of objects in your bucket and maybe you've been providing tags on those objects as you've been uploading them, and now that those files have been processed, maybe you want to go ahead and change those tags and maybe we're thinking about hundreds, thousands or even millions of objects.
- Now, of course, a bucket policy doesn't have to always make an object publicly accessible where we had the star back there for the principle, well, we can have roles, we can have our internal groups, we can have users, and we would only allow authenticated users within our account access to our bucket.

- Transcript signals that so again, storage classes are assigned per object and not the entire bucket.
- Transcript signals that storage classes are defined at the object level and not the bucket level.

- Transcript signals that so, for example, Cloudtrail logs would indicate somebody making a change to a bucket like configuring encryption or changing the storage type, but it wouldn't log an upload made by a user outside of your account or someone on the internet accessing an object from like a gaming application that wouldn't be logged by Cloudtrail event logging, but it would be logged by server access logs.
- Transcript signals that so if we had all of our images inside a prefix called images inside of our bucket and we didn't want to give access to anything else in our bucket except for our images, well, we could change the resource to simply include everything under the prefix of images, and folks on the internet would only be able to retrieve files and objects that lived inside of the images prefix there.
- Transcript signals that so even if we were trying to modify individual buckets and make individual objects or the bucket itself public, even if we changed or turned off block public access per bucket, well, Amazon is still going to block us from making any changes to our policy that permits public access because the account level settings are going to proceed over top of our individual bucket settings.
- Transcript signals that so this may be handy if you're using applications and you have configured some apps to point to a specific bucket or some CI CD pipelines or something like that, you can simply keep the name and then when you drop objects inside of that bucket, then you can obviously have your application or your pipeline continue to use that same bucket.
- Transcript signals that well, we can retrieve a specific version of an object as needed from an S3 bucket that has versioning enabled a regular gets just a traditional get against that object is going to retrieve the latest version if that get also specifies the version of that object, we're going to get that specific version of the object.
- Transcript signals that so again, a little bit more complex, not too crazy, but again, I want to showcase how we continue adding more and more to our policy to get more fine grained controls specifically for our bucket and maybe objects in our bucket and of course limit the actions that a specific user can perform on those resources.
- Transcript signals that so if you have a lot of objects in your bucket and maybe you've been providing tags on those objects as you've been uploading them, and now that those files have been processed, maybe you want to go ahead and change those tags and maybe we're thinking about hundreds, thousands or even millions of objects.
- Transcript signals that now, of course, a bucket policy doesn't have to always make an object publicly accessible where we had the star back there for the principle, well, we can have roles, we can have our internal groups, we can have users, and we would only allow authenticated users within our account access to our bucket.

- Treat Object Storage Patterns as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.
- Treat Block and File Storage Design as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.
- Treat Data Protection and Immutability as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.
- Treat Lifecycle Operations as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.

- Design implication: so again, storage classes are assigned per object and not the entire bucket.
- Design implication: storage classes are defined at the object level and not the bucket level.

- Design implication: so, for example, Cloudtrail logs would indicate somebody making a change to a bucket like configuring encryption or changing the storage type, but it wouldn't log an upload made by a user outside of your account or someone on the internet accessing an object from like a gaming application that wouldn't be logged by Cloudtrail event logging, but it would be logged by server access logs.

#### Module: Core Services and Building Blocks
- Core services should be understood through control planes, data paths, and failure boundaries.
- Primary implementation choices should be explicit enough to compare with Azure and GCP equivalents.

- Now some of these configurations include things like bucket versioning tags, default encryption server access, logging, cloudtrail data events, event notifications, transfer accelerations, object lock requester pays, and static website hosting.
- So because we don't have versioning enabled on this bucket, I'm just simply going to choose move current versions of objects between storage classes.
- Well, we can retrieve a specific version of an object as needed from an S3 bucket that has versioning enabled a regular gets just a traditional get against that object is going to retrieve the latest version if that get also specifies the version of that object, we're going to get that specific version of the object.
- Then when you create an object in Amazon S three, you can choose its class, you can also modify its storage class manually, or as we'll see as well, you can use Amazon S3 Lifecycle configurations to move objects automatically between all these storage classes.
- Now if we were to apply a filter on these objects and we were to look for just the objects that were inside of the website prefix, we would end up with index and leadership because only those two objects meet the filter of Amazon S3 Deepdive slash website.
- So once I upload all of my assets into this bucket and I make all the other changes required in order to make these objects public and all that good stuff, I can simply click on this link directly and it should show my website.
- Another benefit of versioning is it allows us to administer past versions through lifecycle management so we can move or set expiration for objects for a certain number of days after they are no longer the current version.
- So if you scroll down like in this screenshot, you would see all the things we talked about bucket versioning tags, static website hosting, versioning, all that good stuff are all inside of our properties tab here.
- So that's the simplest way to create a lifecycle rule in order to automatically move your objects between storage classes based upon a predefined rule that you create inside of each of these lifecycle rules.
- So by enabling our S3 versioning, we really are equip ourselves with this safeguard against both unintended user actions and application failures with versioning any modification or deletion to an object.
- So if this sounds confusing, don't worry, we're going to go over all this stuff in a future section where we're going to dive deep into versioning buckets and finally moving and copying objects around.

- Transcript signals that now some of these configurations include things like bucket versioning tags, default encryption server access, logging, cloudtrail data events, event notifications, transfer accelerations, object lock requester pays, and static website hosting.
- Transcript signals that so because we don't have versioning enabled on this bucket, I'm just simply going to choose move current versions of objects between storage classes.
- Transcript signals that well, we can retrieve a specific version of an object as needed from an S3 bucket that has versioning enabled a regular gets just a traditional get against that object is going to retrieve the latest version if that get also specifies the version of that object, we're going to get that specific version of the object.
- Transcript signals that then when you create an object in Amazon S three, you can choose its class, you can also modify its storage class manually, or as we'll see as well, you can use Amazon S3 Lifecycle configurations to move objects automatically between all these storage classes.
- Transcript signals that now if we were to apply a filter on these objects and we were to look for just the objects that were inside of the website prefix, we would end up with index and leadership because only those two objects meet the filter of Amazon S3 Deepdive slash website.
- Transcript signals that so once I upload all of my assets into this bucket and I make all the other changes required in order to make these objects public and all that good stuff, I can simply click on this link directly and it should show my website.
- Transcript signals that another benefit of versioning is it allows us to administer past versions through lifecycle management so we can move or set expiration for objects for a certain number of days after they are no longer the current version.
- Transcript signals that so if you scroll down like in this screenshot, you would see all the things we talked about bucket versioning tags, static website hosting, versioning, all that good stuff are all inside of our properties tab here.
- Transcript signals that so that's the simplest way to create a lifecycle rule in order to automatically move your objects between storage classes based upon a predefined rule that you create inside of each of these lifecycle rules.
- Transcript signals that so by enabling our S3 versioning, we really are equip ourselves with this safeguard against both unintended user actions and application failures with versioning any modification or deletion to an object.
- Transcript signals that so if this sounds confusing, don't worry, we're going to go over all this stuff in a future section where we're going to dive deep into versioning buckets and finally moving and copying objects around.

particular element within a database This Record relates to another record in a different table so it lets us get very efficient with how we store the data and then we have documents we think about semi-structured and this is often self-describing so there isn't a traditional schema but what we'll have is something like an XML file or a Json file so it describes its own structure within it but we can have a mixture of different structures within a particular document so these are very common types of data um that we're going to see today now another thing we might see as part of these is we - Design implication: now some of these configurations include things like bucket versioning tags, default encryption server access, logging, cloudtrail data events, event notifications, transfer accelerations, object lock requester pays, and static website hosting.
- Design implication: so because we don't have versioning enabled on this bucket, I'm just simply going to choose move current versions of objects between storage classes.
- Design implication: well, we can retrieve a specific version of an object as needed from an S3 bucket that has versioning enabled a regular gets just a traditional get against that object is going to retrieve the latest version if that get also specifies the version of that object, we're going to get that specific version of the object.
- Design implication: then when you create an object in Amazon S three, you can choose its class, you can also modify its storage class manually, or as we'll see as well, you can use Amazon S3 Lifecycle configurations to move objects automatically between all these storage classes.

#### Module: Operational and Architecture Patterns
- Patterns matter when scale, governance, or reliability turns basic service use into system design.
- Prefer design implications and trade-offs over memorizing isolated features.

- Now some of these configurations include things like bucket versioning tags, default encryption server access, logging, cloudtrail data events, event notifications, transfer accelerations, object lock requester pays, and static website hosting.
- Now maybe you only want a single version of an object or you know, maybe you want to save on costs and that could be a reason that we want to suspend versioning on our bucket.
- So because we don't have versioning enabled on this bucket, I'm just simply going to choose move current versions of objects between storage classes.
- This is just one architecture with the idea I want you to remember is that it's going to be a more dynamic route where there's going to be tons of requests, and for the static content, things that doesn't change very often, then usually you go through a CDN layer, such as CloudFront to cache a content at the edge and save some costs and improve the performance, there's going to be more for your HTML files your big files your videos and so on.
- In this course in the performance section, there's going to be a recap of the content, distribution and intro to CloudFront and how it increases our performance of our S3 buckets by distributing our contents around the world and in the static website hosting section, we're actually going to demo how we can use CloudFront to secure our website using TLS.
- Well, we can retrieve a specific version of an object as needed from an S3 bucket that has versioning enabled a regular gets just a traditional get against that object is going to retrieve the latest version if that get also specifies the version of that object, we're going to get that specific version of the object.
- Now, of course, a bucket policy doesn't have to always make an object publicly accessible where we had the star back there for the principle, well, we can have roles, we can have our internal groups, we can have users, and we would only allow authenticated users within our account access to our bucket.
- So again, if this box is checked and then me as an administrator, try to go into Amazon S3 console and say, you know what, I'm going to place this new bucket policy, I'm going to edit the existing one or create a new bucket policy that enables either my bucket or objects to be public.
- So bucket policy can allow access to a bucket for our internal users, maybe members of our organization that can have access into our AWS accounts and bucket policies can also provide access to objects that are publicly available, meaning anybody can get access to it.
- Now, with a perfect blend of engaging lectures, demos and challenges, we're going to unravel how Amazon S3 works, how you can optimize performance, reduce costs and, yes, secure your data using all the amazing S3 security features.
- So once I upload all of my assets into this bucket and I make all the other changes required in order to make these objects public and all that good stuff, I can simply click on this link directly and it should show my website.

- Transcript signals that now some of these configurations include things like bucket versioning tags, default encryption server access, logging, cloudtrail data events, event notifications, transfer accelerations, object lock requester pays, and static website hosting.
- Transcript signals that now maybe you only want a single version of an object or you know, maybe you want to save on costs and that could be a reason that we want to suspend versioning on our bucket.
- Transcript signals that so because we don't have versioning enabled on this bucket, I'm just simply going to choose move current versions of objects between storage classes.
- Transcript signals that this is just one architecture with the idea I want you to remember is that it's going to be a more dynamic route where there's going to be tons of requests, and for the static content, things that doesn't change very often, then usually you go through a CDN layer, such as CloudFront to cache a content at the edge and save some costs and improve the performance, there's going to be more for your HTML files your big files your videos and so on.
- Transcript signals that in this course in the performance section, there's going to be a recap of the content, distribution and intro to CloudFront and how it increases our performance of our S3 buckets by distributing our contents around the world and in the static website hosting section, we're actually going to demo how we can use CloudFront to secure our website using TLS.
- Transcript signals that well, we can retrieve a specific version of an object as needed from an S3 bucket that has versioning enabled a regular gets just a traditional get against that object is going to retrieve the latest version if that get also specifies the version of that object, we're going to get that specific version of the object.
- Transcript signals that now, of course, a bucket policy doesn't have to always make an object publicly accessible where we had the star back there for the principle, well, we can have roles, we can have our internal groups, we can have users, and we would only allow authenticated users within our account access to our bucket.
- Transcript signals that so again, if this box is checked and then me as an administrator, try to go into Amazon S3 console and say, you know what, I'm going to place this new bucket policy, I'm going to edit the existing one or create a new bucket policy that enables either my bucket or objects to be public.
- Transcript signals that so bucket policy can allow access to a bucket for our internal users, maybe members of our organization that can have access into our AWS accounts and bucket policies can also provide access to objects that are publicly available, meaning anybody can get access to it.
- Transcript signals that now, with a perfect blend of engaging lectures, demos and challenges, we're going to unravel how Amazon S3 works, how you can optimize performance, reduce costs and, yes, secure your data using all the amazing S3 security features.
- Transcript signals that so once I upload all of my assets into this bucket and I make all the other changes required in order to make these objects public and all that good stuff, I can simply click on this link directly and it should show my website.

- Design implication: now some of these configurations include things like bucket versioning tags, default encryption server access, logging, cloudtrail data events, event notifications, transfer accelerations, object lock requester pays, and static website hosting.
- Design implication: now maybe you only want a single version of an object or you know, maybe you want to save on costs and that could be a reason that we want to suspend versioning on our bucket.

- Design implication: this is just one architecture with the idea I want you to remember is that it's going to be a more dynamic route where there's going to be tons of requests, and for the static content, things that doesn't change very often, then usually you go through a CDN layer, such as CloudFront to cache a content at the edge and save some costs and improve the performance, there's going to be more for your HTML files your big files your videos and so on.

### Phase 2 - Core Services
- Amazon Glacier should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon Lex should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- AWS Config should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon S3 should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- AWS SAM should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.

### Phase 3 - Advanced Patterns
- Pattern emphasis: Block and File Storage Design becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Cost and Performance Optimization becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Data Protection and Immutability becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Hybrid Transfer and Mobility becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Lifecycle Operations becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.

### Phase 4 - System Design (Production)
#### Reference Architecture
- Design s3 foundations with explicit service boundaries, operational controls, and recovery expectations.
#### Scaling Considerations
- Scaling risk appears when s3 foundations decisions are made service-by-service without a whole-system operating model.
#### Cost Considerations
- The main cost driver is usually misalignment between service choice, operations, and workload behavior.

## Cross-Domain Referrals

- No secondary referral sources were required for this topic.

### File: outputs/AWS Speciality/Security/06_Domain 4 - Identity and Access Management/156_[SAA_DVA_SOA] S3 CORS.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now let's talk about CORS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: CORS is Cross-Origin Resource Sharing.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And this is something you need to know at the exam for one question.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, I'm gonna go in depth to how that works and that will make answering that question very easy.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, the origin is a scheme, a protocol a host, a domain, and a port.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For example, if you look at https://www.example.com the implied port is 443 for HTTPS, the protocol is HTTPS itself and the domain of course is www.example.com Now, CORS is a web browser based mechanism of security to allow or to deny request to other origins while visiting the main origin.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: So, let's have a look at what the same origin means.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: So, it's the same origin if we have the same scheme, the same host and the same port.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: For example, we have these two URLs, they're share the same origin.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But, we can have different origins.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon EBS
- Key Insights: For example, www.example.com and other.example.com And so, if our web browser is visiting one website and supposed to make a request to another website as part of the request scheme then these requests will not be fulfilled unless the other origin allows for the request using the CORS header.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: They're called the Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, let's go through a diagram to understand how that works and how they can be leveraged.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, we have a web server and that's your origin and it's https://www.example.com and a web browser.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And the second web server, which is the cross-origin, okay?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, www.other.com Now, the web browser is going to do an HTTPS request into the first origin web server.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And as part of the result the index that HTML file retrieved is gonna say, Hey, you must also get some images, for example, from this other web server.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, the web browser has a security built in, and first is going to do a pre-flight request to the cross-origin.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, it's gonna say, Hey, I wanna get the options for www.other.com And, by the way, my origin where the request is coming from is the https://www.example.com.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS Config
- Key Insights: And then the web server, if it's considered and configured to use the Cross-Origin Resource Sharing is going to say, Yes, I do allow this origin, the example.com origin, for the get, put and delete methods.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, this is what we call the CORS headers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And, if the web browser is happy with this CORS headers, then the web browser is going to make a request to the other server to retrieve these files and make these calls.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So, how does it apply to Amazon S3?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Well, if a client makes a cross-origin request on our S3 buckets, we need to enable the correct CORS headers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It's a very popular exam question, and one way to do it very quickly is to allow for a specific origin or to allow for*, that means all origins.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, let's have a look.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon EBS, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: We have a web browser and we have an S3 bucket, which has a static website being enabled.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, that's my bucket HTML.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And we are storing our assets, our images in another S3 bucket called my-buckets-assets.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon EBS
- Key Insights: And again, we have enabled a static website for it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon EBS, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So, the web browser goes to the first S3 bucket and say, Hey, I want to get the index, that HTML file, for this URL right here which is, of course, the static website URL.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon EBS
- Key Insights: So, we get back the index.html and within the index.html there is an image and that image lives on another website.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 33:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon EBS
- Key Insights: So, we get the images/coffee this is the web browser doing its thing, but this time it's saying, Hey the target host is this other website, but the origin is this first website.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 34:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: AWS Config, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And if the S3 bucket is not configured to have the correct CORS headers it's going to refuse the request, or else if it's allowing this request, then it's going to have the correct headers and say, Okay, you can do this request and you can get the image.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 35:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So remember, CORS is a web browser security that allows you to enable images or assets or files being retrieved from one S3 bucket in case the request is originating from another origin.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, that's it.

### File: outputs/AWS Speciality/Security/07_Domain 5 - Data Protection/197_Large File Upload to S3 with KMS Key.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So here is a tricky use case for an exam question, and that is quite unintuitive but makes sense when I explain it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So how do you upload a large file to Amazon S3 that is KMS encrypted?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So when you upload a large file, of course you must use a multi-part upload.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And then what permissions do we need for that multi-part upload?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon ECR
- Key Insights: Well, number one, we want to generate a data key, because while we want to make sure we can encrypt the file and encrypt each specific part, but then we also need the KMS decrypt permission to be able to decrypt these parts before they can be reassembled, and then re-encrypt it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And that's the trick.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon ECR
- Key Insights: We're missing the decrypt option.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, let's take an example.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: With a user with the proper permissions, there is a large file.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It does get split up into multiple parts.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Each part is going to be encrypted with its own data key.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Each key is going to be... each file encrypted is going to be uploaded into Amazon S3.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon ECR, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Amazon S3 will use the decrypt operation to decrypt each file into plain text and then recombine them into an S3 object.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And this S3 object is going to be again encrypted into the final object.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Funky, right?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon ECR, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: But what you have to remember is that you need two kind of permissions, including decrypt, for doing a large file upload to Amazon S3 with KMS encryption.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 18:

Line 19:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: As I upload a new object to Amazon S3 into my bucket here, if I read that object immediately, I'm going to always get the latest version of that object because, well, that's the new object.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Recently, Amazon also introduced what they call strong consistency for updates as well.

Line 21:

- Services: Amazon S3

Line 22:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So if I had an object up there and I uploaded a new version of that object and I read that immediately, I may not get that updated version.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: I may actually get the older version because Amazon S3 still needed to replicate that new version across all of the devices, whereas stored from already.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But now what they've introduced is strong consistency for updates.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So as soon as I write an update to my object and I read that immediately, Amazon guarantees that I always get the latest version of that object.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you don't have to worry about eventual consistency anymore.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That's an older concept.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You always have strong consistency for writing new objects and actually updating existing objects.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

- Services: AWS SAM

- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Now S3 is a little bit unique where we have to have that globally unique name and because of that the Arn can simply be Arn, AWS, S3, Amazon S3, Deepdive without the account number.

- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

Line 35:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 37:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It can't be shorter than three.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Bucket names can only consist of lowercase letters, numbers, dots and hyphens.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 39:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can't use an uppercase letter, you cannot use an underscore lowercase numbers, dots, hyphens.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 40:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That's what you can use to make up your bucket name.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 41:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Bucket names must begin and end with a letter or number so we can't begin a bucket name with a dot or a dash.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Constraint or limitation signal; Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 42:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We can't end it with a dot or a dash has to end and begin with a letter or number.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 43:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Bucket names must not contain two adjacent periods, two dots in a row.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 59:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3

Line 60:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, this is something that was new in 2023.

Line 61:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So up until 2023, Amazon did not automatically encrypt your object when they were uploaded.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 62:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now for a while you had the ability to set a default encryption for a bucket and then before that there was actually no default on there, right?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 63:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So anytime you uploaded an object to S3, you had to individually choose if you want to encrypt that object or not.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 64:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now at the beginning of 2023, all new objects uploaded are automatically encrypted.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 65:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Now the default that Amazon S3 is going to use is going to be server side encryption, SSE using Amazon S3 managed Keys.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 66:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So that's where you see the Green SSC Dash S3 and that's going to be our base level encryption for every bucket.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 67:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you want to use KMS, which I generally recommend, you can actually change the default encryption to SSE dash kms and that's if you want more control over your keys, if you want rotation or be able to modify policies or those kind of things.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 68:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability
- Services: (none explicit)

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 70:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: AWS Config, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So continuing on with default encryption, if a put request headers don't include encryption information, then Amazon S3 is going to use that buckets default encryption configuration in order to automatically encrypt our objects.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 71:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3

- Services: (none explicit)

Line 74:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns

- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

Line 5:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

Line 6:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

- Key Insights: Now, this feature is probably going to be pretty handy for you if you regularly share large volumes of data with partners and collaborators, instead of you having to pay for all that data transfer each time that your partners access the data, those costs are actually billed to the requester AWS account instead.

Line 14:

Line 26:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

Line 27:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 28:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 29:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Again, an explicit deny always overrules and allow if there are no rules that allow access.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3

Line 31:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

Line 15:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well, if we enable the feature called object ownership on a bucket, it will actually go ahead and take ownership of every object in the bucket.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 17:

- Services: (none explicit)

Line 18:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now the bucket owner owns every single object in the bucket and it can exclusively manage objects using our bucket policies.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So we have a couple options to control ownership of our objects.

Line 20:

- Services: (none explicit)

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: ACLs are going to be disabled when we enable this and the bucket owner automatically owns every object that is uploaded into that bucket.

Line 22:

Line 23:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

- Services: (none explicit)

Line 2:

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon ECR
- Key Insights: Now this ensures that data is secure and it's almost impossible to decrypt without that specific key.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Protection and Immutability, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, object specific keys are going to be encrypted using that master key like I talked about before, using envelope encryption.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this adds that extra layer of security and it keeps the stored data safe, even if the object specific key becomes compromised.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, the object specific key, they're relatively small.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 7:

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon ECR

Line 9:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: When the master key is rotated and it leaves the original data untouched.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Old versions of the keys are discarded, which further enhances security.

- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

Line 9:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

Line 10:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now when we send unencrypted data from our data center, what it's going to do is going to upload our data, it's going to encrypt our data with the desired key.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3

Line 14:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 15:

Line 16:

Line 17:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3

Line 18:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

Line 19:

- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

### File: outputs/AWS Storage and DB/amazon-s3-deep-dive/09_Integrating with CloudFront for Content Distribution/098_Restricting Access to an Amazon S3 Origin with Origin Access Control (OAC).txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: All right.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So one of the topics that I wanted to make sure we cover in this course is the CloudFront Origin Access Control.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: All right.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So even when you're using a CloudFront distribution to deliver content, which are the objects in your S3 bucket, they may still be directly accessible.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Now, this can pose a security risk for your organization, especially if you've applied extra security controls to the content that's being served through CloudFront and you want to intend to restrict access directly to S3.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Now, to address this concern, AWS offers OAC, which is Origin Access Control, which is a feature that enhances security by ensuring that only specific CloudFront distributions are allowed to access the content that lives in your S3 bucket.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Now, by implementing OAC, you can now prevent direct access to the S3 bucket and ensuring that users can only access the content through your CloudFront distribution.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That way you force users through your CloudFront distribution and all of those additional security controls are enforced on the CloudFront distribution and they will be applied to all of your users.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's take a look at an example.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3

### File: outputs/aws-certified-developer-associate-dva-c01/06_EC2 Instance Storage/049_AMI Overview.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: Now let's talk about what powers our EC2 instances which is an AMI, so AMI stands for Amazon machine image and they represent a customization of an EC2 instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you can use AMIs you created by AWS or you can customize it into your own and what is in an AMI?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS Config, Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: Well we have our own software configuration, we can define and set up the operating system, we can set up any monitoring tool and if we create our own AMI we're going to get a faster boot time and configuration time because all the software that we want to install onto our EC2 instance is going to be prepackaged through the AMI.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So we have to build our own AMIs and they can be built for a specific region and then they can be copied across region if we wanted to use it and leverage the AWS global infrastructure.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So we can launch EC2 instances from different kind of AMIs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: What we've been doing so far in this course is to use a public AMI and these are provided by AWS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS Config, Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So the Amazon Linux 2 AMI is a very popular AMI for AWS and it was provided by AWS themselves but we can create our own AMI then therefore you have to make and maintain them yourself there are tools obviously to automate this but this is a task that you have to do as a cloud user or finally you can launch an EC2 instance from an AWS Marketplace AMI which is an AMI that has been made by someone else and potentially sold by someone else so it is quite common to have vendors on AWS to create their own AMIs or their own software with a nice configuration and so on and then they will sell it through the marketplace AMI for you to buy it and to save some time.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And even you as a user you could create a business of selling AMIs on the AWS marketplace, this is something that some businesses do.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: Okay so the AMI process from an EC2 instance, how does it work?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: Well we'll start an EC2 instance and we'll customize it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Lifecycle Operations
- Services: Amazon EBS
- Key Insights: Then we will stop the instance to make sure the data integrity is correct, then we can build an AMI from it so this will also create EBS snapshots behind the scenes and then finally we can launch instances from other AMIs so this is what we'll be doing in the demo and the next lecture.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: So we have US-EAST-1A, and we can create the same instance as US-EAST-1B, so the process is we launch the instance in US-EAST-1A, we're going to customize it then we're going to create an AMI from it this will be our custom AMI.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: And then in US-EAST-1B we will be able to launch from that AMI and we'll effectively create a copy of our EC2 instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So I hope you're excited and I will see you in the next lecture.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

### File: outputs/aws-certified-developer-associate-dva-c01/06_EC2 Instance Storage/051_EC2 Instance Store.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Cost and Performance Optimization
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So we've seen a way to attach a network drive onto our EC2 Instances but they have limited performance and I said with cause because it's a really good performance but sometimes you want something even higher performance and that is going to be a hardware disk attached onto you EC2 Instance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Storage economics signal.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So the EC2 Instance is a virtual machine but it is obviously attached to a real hardware server.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And some of these servers do have disk space that is attached directly you know, with a physical connection onto the server.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: And so a special type of EC2 Instance can leverage something called an EC2 Instance Store, which is the name of the hardware, the hard drive attached to the physical server.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Cost and Performance Optimization
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So what we do with EC2 Instance Store, we use them for better I/O performance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Storage economics signal.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Cost and Performance Optimization
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We also make sure that they have good through put and so on, so they're a great choice when you want to have extremely high disk performance.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Storage economics signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: But the caveat is that if you stop or you terminate your EC2 Instance, that has an Instance Store, then the storage will be lost.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: And therefore it's called an ephemeral storage so that means that the EC2 Instance Store can not be used as a durable long term place to store your data.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So what is a good use case for it then?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well if you have a buffer, a cache, you want to have scratch data or temporary content, this would be a great place to do these things but not for long term storage.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design
- Services: Amazon EBS
- Key Insights: For long term storage, EBS for example is a great use case.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: Finally, in case the on the line server of the EC2 Instance does fail, then you'll risk to have a dear loss because the hardware attached to the EC2 Instance will fail as well.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So if you do decide to use an EC2 Instance Store, then it is your entire responsibility to make sure that you back it up and that you replicate it correctly based on your needs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Cost and Performance Optimization
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So what I mean by better performance, this is just an example to illustrate it, don't need to know it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Storage economics signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But if you look at for example, the Instance size of I3 dot something, there is an Instance Store attached to these kinds of instances and if you look at the Read IOPS and the Write IOPS which correspond to how many I/O operations we can do per second.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then you can see at some of these random Read IOPS and Write IOPS can reach 3.3 million or 1.4 million, for the most performant one.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design
- Services: Amazon EBS
- Key Insights: And to put this in comparison with an EBS volume of type BP2 for example, you can reach thirty two thousand IOPS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is a lot more.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Cost and Performance Optimization
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: But again, it's just to illustrate my point from an exam perspective anytime you see very high performance hardware attached volume for your EC2 Instances, think local EC2 Instance Store.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Storage economics signal.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That's it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 53:

Line 54:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

Line 55:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

### File: outputs/aws-certified-developer-associate-dva-c01/11_Amazon S3 Introduction/116_S3 Security_ Bucket Policy.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So now let's talk about Amazon S3-Security.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The first part is User-Based.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So as a user you can have IAM policies that you and this IAM policy is going to authorize which API calls should be allowed for a specific IAM user.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You can also have Resource-Based Security.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is new.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: We can use what's called S3 Bucket policies and there are bucket wide rules that you can assign directly from the S3 console.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And this will allow, for example, a specific user to come in or allow a user from another account, this is called cross-account to access your S3 Buckets.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: This is also how we'll make our S3 Buckets public as I will show you in a minute.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Next, you have the Object Access Control List or ACL, they're finer grain security and they can be disabled.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And if you need to go at the Bucket level, you can have Buckets ACL which is way less common also can be disabled.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 5:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, the origin is a scheme, a protocol a host, a domain, and a port.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For example, if you look at https://www.example.com the implied port is 443 for HTTPS, the protocol is HTTPS itself and the domain of course is www.example.com Now, CORS is a web browser based mechanism of security to allow or to deny request to other origins while visiting the main origin.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: So, let's have a look at what the same origin means.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: So, it's the same origin if we have the same scheme, the same host and the same port.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: For example, we have these two URLs, they're share the same origin.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But, we can have different origins.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Block and File Storage Design, Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon EBS
- Key Insights: For example, www.example.com and other.example.com And so, if our web browser is visiting one website and supposed to make a request to another website as part of the request scheme then these requests will not be fulfilled unless the other origin allows for the request using the CORS header.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: They're called the Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 14:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

Line 15:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 16:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, www.other.com Now, the web browser is going to do an HTTPS request into the first origin web server.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 18:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 19:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

- Key Insights: So, it's gonna say, Hey, I wanna get the options for www.other.com And, by the way, my origin where the request is coming from is the https://www.example.com.

- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, the idea is that when you generate a pre-signed URL, the user that will get that URL will inherit the permissions of the user that generated that URL for a GET or a PUT.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So what's the use case?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Well, say you have an S3 bucket and it's private, and you want to give someone outside of AWS access to one file.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You don't want to make that file public.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You don't want to make that file in any way that would break your security, right?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So, you as your bucket owner or the user, you're going to generate a pre-signed URL out of this file, and the S3 Bucket will give you a URL.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That URL is going to be pre-signed, meaning that it will carry over your credentials, in terms of authorization to access that file.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then you will send this URL to the target user that you want to give access to the file for a limited amount of time.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 12:

- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And then that user, sorry, will be using the URL to access the file on the S3 Buckets.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Access-path and data-plane signal.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, pre-signed URL are a very, very common use case when it comes to temporary access to one specific file for download or even for upload.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Object Storage Patterns
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So, the examples are to only allow logged-in users to download a premium video of your S3 bucket or to allow an ever-changing list of users to download files by generating URLs dynamically, or to allow, temporarily, a user to upload a file to a precise location in your S3 bucket, while maintaining your S3 bucket private.

- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: Now let's talk about what powers our EC2 instances which is an AMI, so AMI stands for Amazon machine image and they represent a customization of an EC2 instance.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context

Line 3:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS Config, Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: Well we have our own software configuration, we can define and set up the operating system, we can set up any monitoring tool and if we create our own AMI we're going to get a faster boot time and configuration time because all the software that we want to install onto our EC2 instance is going to be prepackaged through the AMI.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So we have to build our own AMIs and they can be built for a specific region and then they can be copied across region if we wanted to use it and leverage the AWS global infrastructure.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So we can launch EC2 instances from different kind of AMIs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: What we've been doing so far in this course is to use a public AMI and these are provided by AWS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: AWS Config, Amazon EC2
- Key Insights: So the Amazon Linux 2 AMI is a very popular AMI for AWS and it was provided by AWS themselves but we can create our own AMI then therefore you have to make and maintain them yourself there are tools obviously to automate this but this is a task that you have to do as a cloud user or finally you can launch an EC2 instance from an AWS Marketplace AMI which is an AMI that has been made by someone else and potentially sold by someone else so it is quite common to have vendors on AWS to create their own AMIs or their own software with a nice configuration and so on and then they will sell it through the marketplace AMI for you to buy it and to save some time.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Storage Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And even you as a user you could create a business of selling AMIs on the AWS marketplace, this is something that some businesses do.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 9:

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 10:

- Services: Amazon EC2

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes storage behavior detail.

Line 11:

Line 12:

- Services: AWS SAM
